card.intuition: Bayesian bandit: maintain a Beta(α, β) posterior over each arm's success probability. At each round, sample θᵢ ~ Beta(αᵢ, βᵢ) for all arms, then pull arm with highest sample. Update: on reward 1 (success) increment α; on reward 0 (failure) increment β. Naturally balances exploration (uncertain arms have wide Beta → high chance of large sample) and exploitation.
card.analogy: Hiring the best candidate by running a mental lottery: for each applicant, draw a score from your current belief about their ability (Beta distribution). The applicant with the highest draw gets the position today. After evaluating them, update your belief — a bad hire widens your uncertainty back, a good one narrows it.
when_to_use: Often outperforms UCB1 empirically for ad selection and A/B testing
Extends naturally to contextual bandits and Bayesian deep RL
gotchas: Beta-Bernoulli conjugacy only for binary rewards — use Gaussian Thompson Sampling for continuous rewards
Tends to under-explore early when priors are informative — use diffuse prior Beta(1,1) to start
Computationally simple but lacks UCB's worst-case regret guarantees

exercise.prompt: Implement Thompson Sampling for Bernoulli bandits.
hints: Select the arm whose sampled theta is highest — argmax over the list of samples.
exercise.test_code:
import random
random.seed(99)

# Arm 1 has p=0.8, others p=0.2
probs = [0.2, 0.8, 0.2]
bandit = ThompsonBandit(k=3)

for _ in range(600):
    arm = bandit.select()
    reward = 1 if random.random() < probs[arm] else 0
    bandit.update(arm, reward)

# Best arm (1) should have most successes
assert bandit.alpha[1] > bandit.alpha[0]
assert bandit.alpha[1] > bandit.alpha[2]
# Posterior mean for arm 1 should be close to 0.8
mean1 = bandit.alpha[1] / (bandit.alpha[1] + bandit.beta[1])
assert mean1 > 0.65, f'Posterior mean for best arm: {mean1:.3f}'
print('PASS')
